title: Chapter 1 - The Web vs. The Internet
description: Quick concept check on internet vs web, and the client/server idea.

questions.prompt: Which statement is the most accurate?

options.label: The internet is only Wi‑Fi, the web is only mobile data.
The web is cables and routers, the internet is websites.
The internet is the network, and the web is a service that runs on it.
The web and the internet are exactly the same thing.

explanations.overview: The internet is the global network, and the web is a system of websites that uses that network.

byOptionId.d: They’re related, but not the same: the web runs on the internet.
byOptionId.c: Correct. The web is one thing that uses the internet.
byOptionId.b: That’s flipped: cables/routers are part of the internet.
byOptionId.a: Wi‑Fi and mobile data are ways to connect, not definitions of web/internet.

questions.prompt: When you visit a website, what is usually the **client**?

options.label: The database that stores the website
The server computer that hosts the site

explanations.overview: The client is the program that asks for data (your browser), and the server is the program/computer that answers.

byOptionId.c: Correct. The browser requests pages and data.
byOptionId.a: A database helps the server, but it’s not usually the client.
byOptionId.d: That’s the server side: it answers requests.
byOptionId.b: Hardware input devices aren’t clients in this sense.

questions.prompt: What happens first when loading a web page?

options.label: The server asks your browser for permission to exist.
The browser guesses the page without contacting the internet.
The server sends a response before the browser asks.
The browser sends a request to a server.

explanations.overview: The web is mostly request → response. The client asks first.

byOptionId.c: A response is usually a reply to a request.
byOptionId.d: Correct. The browser requests something like `/` or `/index.html`.
byOptionId.b: Browsers can cache, but normally they still work through requests and responses.
byOptionId.a: Servers don’t do that; clients initiate most page loads.

questions.prompt: What service turns a domain like `example.com` into an IP address?

explanations.overview: DNS resolves domain names to IP addresses so browsers can connect.

byOptionId.d: Correct — DNS maps names to IP addresses.
byOptionId.c: An HTTP server responds to requests but doesn't map names to IPs.
byOptionId.b: Browsers use DNS but aren't the mapping service themselves.
byOptionId.a: FTP is a file transfer protocol, not name resolution.
